section: Optimization of Process Conditions for Ultralightweight Steel with More Than 13 wt% of Al and 5 wt% of Cr

claims.subject: Fe–30Mn–13.2Al–1.6C–5Cr ultralightweight steel
claims.claim: The steel has the composition Fe–30Mn–13.2Al–1.6C–5Cr (wt%), contains more than 13 wt% Al, and reduces density by 20%.
claims.evidence_text: An ultralightweight Fe–30Mn–13.2Al–1.6C–5Cr steel, which contains more than 13 wt% of Al and thereby reduces the density by 20%, is developed.

claims.subject: ultralightweight steel
claims.claim: The steel is very brittle due to high Al content.
claims.evidence_text: The ultralightweight steel, which is very brittle due to high Al content,

claims.subject: hot rolling
claims.claim: Hot rolling was conducted after soaking at temperatures in the range 1100–1200 °C for 2 h.
claims.evidence_text: Hot rolling is conducted after soaking at the temperature range of 1100–1200 °C for 2 h.

claims.subject: specimen soaked at 1100 °C
claims.claim: The specimen soaked at 1100 °C was hot-rolled successfully.
claims.evidence_text: The ultralightweight steel is hot-rolled successfully after soaking at 1100 °C

claims.subject: specimens soaked at 1150 and 1200 °C
claims.claim: Specimens soaked at 1150 and 1200 °C were intergranularly cracked after hot rolling, due to coarse grain and a large fraction and size of ferrite transformed to ordered DO3 phase at grain boundaries during cooling.

values.root causes: coarse grain, large fraction/size of ferrite transforming to ordered DO3 at grain boundaries

claims.evidence_text: specimens soaked at 1150 and 1200 °C are intergranularly cracked after hot rolling, resulting from coarse grain and a large fraction and size of ferrite, which is transformed to ordered ${\mathsf { D O }} _ {3}$ phase during cooling, at the grain boundaries.

claims.subject: homogenization heat treatment
claims.claim: Homogenization heat treatment involved holding at 1050 °C for 2 h, followed by either water quenching or air cooling.
claims.evidence_text: In homogenization heat treatment, water quenching and air cooling are performed, respectively, after holding at 1050 °C for 2 h.

claims.subject: air-cooled steel
claims.claim: Air-cooled steel exhibited inferior tensile property due to the formation of brittle ordered DO3 phase at grain boundaries.
claims.evidence_text: The air-cooled steel has inferior tensile property due to the formation of brittle ordered ${\mathsf { D O }} _ {3}$ phase at grain boundaries.

claims.subject: water-quenched steel
claims.claim: The water-quenched steel showed excellent tensile properties attributed to a uniform microstructure comprising austenite, fine κ-carbide in austenite, and a very small fraction of ordered DO3 phase.
claims.evidence_text: the water-quenched steel shows an excellent tensile property, which is attributed to a uniform microstructure comprising austenite, fine κ-carbide in austenite, and a very small fraction of the ordered ${\mathsf { D O }} _ {3}$ phase.

section: Optimization of Process Conditions for Ultralightweight Steel with More Than 13 wt% of Al and 5 wt% of Cr

claims.subject: Ultralightweight steel
claims.claim: Fe–30Mn–13.2Al–1.6C–5Cr steel contains more than 13 wt% Al and reduces density by 20%
claims.evidence_text: An ultralightweight Fe–30Mn–13.2Al–1.6C–5Cr steel, which contains more than 13 wt% of Al and thereby reduces the density by 20%, is developed.

claims.subject: Hot rolling soaking
claims.claim: Soaking at 1100–1200 °C for 2 h before hot rolling; successful at 1100 °C, intergranular cracking at 1150 and 1200 °C
claims.evidence_text: Hot rolling is conducted after soaking at the temperature range of 1100–1200 °C for 2 h. The ultralightweight steel is hot-rolled successfully after soaking at 1100 °C, whereas specimens soaked at 1150 and 1200 °C are intergranularly cracked after hot rolling, resulting from coarse grain and a large fraction and size of ferrite, which is transformed to ordered DO3 phase during cooling, at the grain boundaries.

claims.subject: Intergranular cracking after hot rolling
claims.claim: Caused by coarse grain and large fraction/size of ferrite at grain boundaries that transforms to ordered DO3 phase during cooling

values.cause: coarse grain + large ferrite transformed to DO3 at grain boundaries

claims.evidence_text: resulting from coarse grain and a large fraction and size of ferrite, which is transformed to ordered DO3 phase during cooling, at the grain boundaries.

claims.subject: Homogenization heat treatment
claims.claim: Homogenization at 1050 °C for 2 h followed by water quenching or air cooling
claims.evidence_text: In homogenization heat treatment, water quenching and air cooling are performed, respectively, after holding at 1050 °C for 2 h.

claims.subject: Air-cooled steel
claims.claim: Inferior tensile property due to formation of brittle ordered DO3 phase at grain boundaries

values.reason: brittle ordered DO3 at grain boundaries

claims.evidence_text: The air-cooled steel has inferior tensile property due to the formation of brittle ordered DO3 phase at grain boundaries.

claims.subject: Water-quenched steel
claims.claim: Excellent tensile property, attributed to uniform microstructure of austenite, fine κ-carbide in austenite, and very small fraction of ordered DO3 phase

values.microstructure: austenite + fine κ-carbide + minimal ordered DO3

claims.evidence_text: Meanwhile, the water-quenched steel shows an excellent tensile property, which is attributed to a uniform microstructure comprising austenite, fine κ-carbide in austenite, and a very small fraction of the ordered DO3 phase.

claims.subject: Brittle phases in Fe–Mn–Al–C lightweight steels
claims.claim: κ-carbide of (Fe, Mn)3AlC type, ferrite, and ordered phases such as B2 or DO3 at grain boundaries are brittle phases that can easily form
claims.evidence_text: κ-carbide of (Fe, Mn)3AlC type, ferrite, and ordered phases such as B2 or DO3 at grain boundaries, which are brittle phases, can be easily formed,

claims.subject: Study approach
claims.claim: Manufacture ultralightweight Fe–Mn–Al–C steel with >13 wt% Al by suppressing brittle phases through Cr addition and optimization of hot rolling and heat treatment
claims.evidence_text: In this study, it was aimed to manufacture ultralightweight Fe–Mn–Al–C steel containing more than 13 wt% of Al which can reduce 20% of density by suppressing the formation of brittle coarse κ-carbide, ferrite, and ordered phases at grain boundaries through the addition of Cr and optimization of hot rolling and heat treatment conditions.

claims.subject: Fe–30Mn–13.2Al–1.6C–5Cr hot-rolled plates
claims.claim: Plates soaked at 1200 and 1150°C developed cracks after hot rolling, whereas the plate soaked at 1100°C was hot-rolled successfully without cracks.
claims.evidence_text: Both plates soaked at 1200 and 1150 °C had crack after hot rolling, while the plate soaked at 1100 °C was hot-rolled successfully without cracks (Figure 1a).

claims.subject: 1150°C soaked plate
claims.claim: Microstructure consisted of austenite with large grain size and BCC ferrite (ordered DO3) at grain boundaries. Austenite grain size ~60 μm, DO3 area fraction 11.5%, thickness 5.5 μm.
claims.evidence_text: the microstructure of the 1150 °C plate was austenite (face centered cubic (FCC) phase) with large grain size and ferrite (body centered cubic (BCC) phase) at grain boundaries. The BCC phase is identified as ordered DO3 phase by X-ray diffraction (XRD) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis, as shown in Figure 3. The austenite grain size was approximately 60 μm and the average area fraction and thickness of DO3 phase were 11.5% and 5.5 μm, respectively.

claims.subject: Fe–30Mn–13.2Al–1.6C–5Cr steel
claims.claim: No coarse κ-carbide precipitates at grain boundaries despite 13.2 wt% Al, confirming that 5 wt% Cr effectively suppresses coarse κ-carbide.
claims.evidence_text: there is no coarse κ-carbide at grain boundaries in the investigated steel in spite of 13.2 wt% of Al content. It is confirmed that 5 wt% of Cr is effective to prevent precipitation of coarse κ-carbide.

claims.subject: 1150°C soaked plate
claims.claim: Cracking occurred by intergranular fracture along austenite grain boundaries.
claims.evidence_text: The crack of the 1150 °C plate was intergranular fracture along austenite grain boundaries.

claims.subject: 1100°C soaked plate
claims.claim: Austenite grain size 35 μm, DO3 area fraction 8.1%, thickness 2.8 μm at grain boundaries.
claims.evidence_text: the plate soaked at 1100 °C shows relatively fine austenite grain size and small fraction and size of DO3 at grain boundaries (Figure 1c,e). The austenite grain size was 35 μm, and the average area fraction and thickness of DO3 phase were 8.1% and 2.8 μm, respectively.

claims.subject: Fe–Mn–Al–C steels
claims.claim: Between 1100–1200°C, BCC phase fraction increases with temperature; at soaking, austenite + grain-boundary ferrite exists and transforms to ordered DO3 during cooling, leading to large DO3 at higher temperatures and vulnerability to intergranular cracking.
claims.evidence_text: According to the calculated phase diagram of Fe–Mn–Al–C steels in the previous result reporting that BCC phase exists at the soaking temperature between 1100 and 1200 °C and the fraction of BCC phase increases with increasing the soaking temperature, it is expected that the microstructure at soaking temperature is austenite and some ferrite at grain boundaries, which is transformed to ordered DO3 during cooling. Therefore, the plate soaked at higher temperature has a large size of austenite and relatively large fraction and size of DO3 at grain boundaries, which is vulnerable to intergranular cracking.

claims.subject: 1100°C hot-rolled plate after homogenization (1050°C/2h)
claims.claim: Air-cooled specimen: austenite + coarse DO3 (4.2% area, 2.6 μm thick). Water-quenched specimen: uniform austenite matrix with fine κ-carbide and minimal DO3 (1.27% area, 2.4 μm thick).
claims.evidence_text: Austenite and coarse DO3 phase at the grain boundaries were observed in the air-cooled specimen (Figure 2b,d), and the average area fraction and thickness of DO3 phase were 4.2% and 2.6 μm, respectively, whereas uniform microstructure with austenite and small amount of DO3 was observed in the water-quenched specimen (Figure 2c,e), and the average area fraction and thickness of DO3 phase were 1.27% and 2.4 μm, respectively.

claims.subject: Fe–Mn–Al–C steel homogenization
claims.claim: Slow cooling promotes transformation of austenite to ferrite/DO3; fast cooling (water quenching) is required for a uniform microstructure.
claims.evidence_text: It was found that transformation of austenite to ferrite (or DO3 phase) happened at a slow cooling rate, so fast cooling is necessary to have uniform microstructure by preventing ferrite transformation.

claims.subject: κ-carbide in ultralightweight steel with 23 at% Al (13.2 wt%)
claims.claim: κ-carbide forms rapidly without Al diffusion due to high Al concentration; water quenching cannot suppress κ-carbide formation.
claims.evidence_text: Because the Al concentration of this ultralightweight steel was 23 at% (13.2 wt%) of Al, κ-carbide may be formed very quickly without diffusion of Al atoms, so it is easily estimated that water quenching cannot suppress κ-carbide formation in this steel.

claims.subject: BCC (ferrite/DO3) in ultralightweight steel
claims.claim: BCC formation requires longer time than κ-carbide because C and Mn must leave BCC and Al must diffuse to BCC; water quenching can sufficiently suppress BCC formation.
claims.evidence_text: As C and Mn atoms should move from BCC to FCC and Al diffuses to BCC, the formation of BCC requires a longer time compared to that for κ-carbide. Therefore, the formation of BCC can be sufficiently suppressed with water quenching.

claims.subject: water-quenched specimen
claims.claim: XRD and TEM reveal austenite matrix containing fine κ-carbide, and BCC grain-boundary phase is ordered DO3.
claims.evidence_text: In-depth microstructural analysis on water-quenched specimen was conducted using XRD and TEM, as shown in Figure 3, which illustrates that the water-quenched specimen has austenite and fine κ-carbide inside austenite matrix and BCC phase at grain boundaries was identified as ordered DO3 phase.

claims.subject: air-cooled specimen
claims.claim: Tensile specimen fractured in elastic region, showing no measurable yield or tensile strength, due to brittle DO3 at grain boundaries.
claims.evidence_text: The air-cooled specimen shows very poor tensile property. Tensile specimen was fractured at elastic region and even failed to have yield and tensile strength, resulting from brittle ordered DO3 at grain boundaries.

claims.subject: water-quenched specimen
claims.claim: Tensile strength 1048 MPa and total elongation 38% due to uniform austenite matrix with fine κ-carbide and very small DO3 fraction.
claims.evidence_text: the water-quenched specimen shows excellent tensile property with 1048 MPa of tensile strength and 38% of total elongation due to uniform microstructure with austenite and fine κ-carbide inside austenite matrix and very small fraction of DO3 phase.

claims.subject: post-tensile fracture surfaces
claims.claim: Air-cooled specimen: cracks initiate and propagate along DO3 grain boundaries, with no plastic deformation of austenite. Water-quenched specimen: austenite matrix significantly elongated, ductile fracture.
claims.evidence_text: In the air-cooled specimen, the cracks initiated and propagated along DO3 phase at grain boundary and austenite matrix seems not to be plastically deformed, whereas in the water-quenched specimen, austenite matrix was considerably elongated and fractured ductile

claims.subject: water-quenched specimen
claims.claim: exhibits tensile strength of 1048 MPa and total elongation of 38% due to uniform austenite, fine κ-carbide, and minimal DO3 phase
claims.evidence_text: the water-quenched specimen shows excellent tensile property with 1048 MPa of tensile strength and 38% of total elongation due to uniform microstructure with austenite and fine κ-carbide inside austenite matrix and very small fraction of DO3 phase.

claims.subject: air-cooled specimen
claims.claim: cracks initiated and propagated along DO3 phase at grain boundaries, with no plastic deformation of austenite matrix
claims.evidence_text: In the air-cooled specimen, the cracks initiated and propagated along DO3 phase at grain boundary and austenite matrix seems not to be plastically deformed

claims.subject: water-quenched specimen
claims.claim: austenite matrix was considerably elongated and fractured in a ductile manner
claims.evidence_text: austenite matrix was considerably elongated and fractured ductile, which are consistent with stress–strain curves in Figure 4, respectively.

claims.subject: Fe–30Mn–13.2Al–1.6C–5Cr steel
claims.claim: density is 6.30 g cm⁻³, with uniform austenite, fine κ-carbide, and very small ordered DO3 fraction, yielding excellent tensile properties
claims.evidence_text: the ultralightweight Fe–30Mn–13.2Al–1.6C–5Cr steel, density of which is $6 . { \overset { \vartriangle } { 3 0 } } \ g \ c m ^ { \stackrel { \textstyle - } { - } 3 }$, having uniform microstructure with austenite fine κ-carbide inside austenite very small fraction of ordered DO3 phase and excellent tensile property

claims.subject: Fe-30Mn-13.2Al-1.6C-5Cr steel
claims.claim: Steel composition is Fe–30Mn–13.2Al–1.6C–5Cr (wt%).
claims.evidence_text: An ultralightweight steel with Fe–30Mn–13.2Al–1.6C–5Cr (wt%) was designed.

claims.subject: Fe-30Mn-13.2Al-1.6C-5Cr steel
claims.claim: Measured density is 6.30 g cm<sup>-3</sup>.
claims.evidence_text: The measured density of this ultralightweight steel is $6 . 3 0 \ g \mathrm { c m } ^ { - 3 }$ .

claims.subject: Fe-30Mn-13.2Al-1.6C-5Cr steel
claims.claim: Al content increased to 13.2 wt% to reduce density by 20%.
claims.evidence_text: The Al content was increased to 13.2 wt% to reduce the density by 20%.

claims.subject: Fe-30Mn-13.2Al-1.6C-5Cr steel
claims.claim: Mn and C were increased to 30 and 1.6 wt% as austenite stabilizers to prevent ferrite formation from high Al.
claims.evidence_text: To prevent the formation of ferrite due to the increase in Al which is ferrite stabilizing element, Mn and C, austenite stabilizing elements, were increased to 30 and 1.6 wt%, respectively.

claims.subject: Fe-30Mn-13.2Al-1.6C-5Cr steel
claims.claim: 5 wt% Cr added to suppress κ-carbide formation.
claims.evidence_text: Cr (5 wt%) was added to the Fe–30Mn–13.2Al–1.6C steel to suppress κ-carbide formation due to the increase in Al and C.

claims.subject: Fe-Mn-Al-C-Cr steel
claims.claim: Previous study confirmed that Cr retards κ-carbide formation and slightly increases austenite fraction via C enrichment.
claims.evidence_text: In the previous study, it was confirmed that the addition of a certain amount of Cr retards the formation of κ-carbide and thereby increased slightly fraction of austenite due to enrichment of C in austenite.

claims.subject: Fe-30Mn-13.2Al-1.6C-5Cr steel
claims.claim: 5 kg ingot of 30 mm thickness hot-rolled to 7 mm plate.
claims.evidence_text: The 5 kg ingots with 30 mm thickness were hot-rolled to plates with 7 mm thickness and heat-treated for homogenization.

claims.subject: Fe-30Mn-13.2Al-1.6C-5Cr steel
claims.claim: Ingots soaked at 1100, 1150, 1200 °C for 2 h, hot rolled above 1000 °C and air cooled.
claims.evidence_text: The ingots were soaked at three temperatures (1100, 1150, 1200 °C) for 2 h, followed by hot rolling at a temperature higher than 1000 °C and air cooling.

claims.subject: Fe-30Mn-13.2Al-1.6C-5Cr steel
claims.claim: Hot-rolled plates homogenized at 1050 °C for 2 h and cooled by air cooling or water quenching.
claims.evidence_text: The hot-rolled plates were homogenized 1050 °C for 2 h, and then cooled by air cooling and water quenching to room temperature, respectively.

claims.subject: Fe-30Mn-13.2Al-1.6C-5Cr steel homogenization
claims.claim: Homogenization at 1050 °C selected to dissolve κ-carbide and minimize grain coarsening.
claims.evidence_text: Homogenization temperature, 1050 °C, was selected to dissolve κ-carbide as well as to minimize grain coarsening.

claims.subject: Fe-30Mn-13.2Al-1.6C-5Cr steel after optimized treatment
claims.claim: Total elongation of 38% achieved by optimizing hot rolling and heat treatment.
claims.evidence_text: and 38% of total elongation through optimization of hot rolling and heat treatment conditions.

claims.subject: microstructure identification
claims.claim: Microstructure identified using SEM (JSM-7001F), EBSD (NordlysNano detector, AZTEC software), TEM (JEM-2100F), and XRD (DMAX-2500).
claims.evidence_text: Microstructures were identified by SEM (JSM-7001F, JEOL), EBSD (NordlysNano EBSD detector and AZTEC software, Oxford), TEM (JEM-2100F, JEOL), and XRD (DMAX-2500, Rigaku).

claims.subject: SEM sample
claims.claim: SEM specimens mechanically polished and etched with 40% nital (40 mL HNO3 + 100 mL ethyl alcohol).

values.etchant: 40% nital (40 mL HNO3 + 100 mL ethyl alcohol)

claims.evidence_text: Specimens for SEM were mechanically polished and etched using a 40% nital solution (40 mL HNO3 + 100 mL ethyl alcohol).

claims.subject: EBSD sample
claims.claim: EBSD specimens chemomechanically polished with colloidal silica without etching.
claims.evidence_text: Specimens for EBSD were chemomechanically polished with colloidal silica without etching.

claims.subject: XRD measurement
claims.claim: XRD specimen prepared as block 15 mm × 12 mm × 2 mm; measurement with Cu Kα, 40 kV, 200 mA, 2θ range 20°–80°, step 0.02°, 2 s/step.
claims.evidence_text: XRD specimen was prepared as block with 15 mm 12 mm 2 mm after mechanical polishing. XRD measurement was performed with Cu Kα radiation, 40 kV and 200 mA. The diffraction patterns were obtained in step scan mode, 2θ = 20°–80° with a step of 0.02° and 2 s step−1.

claims.subject: TEM sample
claims.claim: TEM discs 3 mm diameter, mechanically polished to 80 μm, electrochemically twin-jet polished with 10% perchloric acid + 90% methyl alcohol.
claims.evidence_text: TEM specimens were prepared as discs with 3 mm in diameter and mechanically polished to 80 μm in thickness and then electrochemically polished in a twin-jet polishing apparatus using a solution (10% perchloric acid þ 90% methyl alcohol).

claims.subject: tensile testing
claims.claim: Tensile tests per ASTM E8 with subsize specimens (2 mm thick, 6 mm wide, 25 mm gauge, 100 mm total) at 2 mm/min crosshead speed, room temperature, on Instron 5882.
claims.evidence_text: Tensile properties were evaluated in accordance of ASTM E8. All tensile tests were conducted with subsize tensile specimens (2 mm in thickness, 6 mm in width, 25 mm in gauge length, and 100 mm in total length) at a cross head speed of 2 mm min−1 at room temperature using universal tensile test machine (INSTRON 5882, INSTRON).
